label: delphi-r2-p6
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The Sierra Vista Municipal Water District (invented) serves ~140,000 people in a chronically drought-stressed service area. After a rate study and a majority-protest hearing that drew protests from ~9% of parcels, the board adopted a "Water Resilience Surcharge": $0.85 per hundred cubic feet on residential usage above Tier 2 (the surcharge falls on roughly the top 22% of residential users), projected to raise $4.1M/year into a restricted fund spent as follows: 55% on turf-replacement and high-efficiency-appliance rebates that prioritize low-income customers; 30% on debt service for a recycled-water plant; 15% on "water ambassador" conservation-education staff.

The rate study concludes that high-tier consumption imposes marginal supply costs roughly 1.6× base-tier costs (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases), but it derives the $0.85 figure in part from what it calls "conservation-incentive pricing" rather than strictly from cost allocation, and the district concedes the rebate program mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge. A ratepayer association has sued, alleging the surcharge exceeds the proportional cost of service attributable to the payers' parcels in violation of article XIII D, section 6(b) of the California Constitution, citing Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano. The district defends the structure under its conservation mandate (Cal. Const. art. X, § 2; Water Code § 375) and argues that, in the alternative, the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all.

## The question

Does the Water Resilience Surcharge violate article XIII D, section 6(b)'s cost-of-service and proportionality requirements? How does Capistrano apply to a tier surcharge that is partly cost-justified (1.6×) and partly incentive-priced, with proceeds partially redistributed as rebates to non-payers? Can article X, section 2's conservation mandate be reconciled with 218, or does 218 win? Is there a colorable argument the surcharge is a tax requiring voter approval (Prop 26), or a non-property-related charge? What is the litigation exposure (refunds, fees) and how could the structure be repaired?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would invalidate the Water Resilience Surcharge, in whole or in substantial part, as violating the cost-of-service requirements of California Constitution article XIII D, section 6(b)."

This is round 2 of a Delphi panel. Your previous answer:

```json
{
  "probability": 70,
  "confidence": "medium",
  "position_summary": "More likely than not a court invalidates at least a substantial part of the surcharge \u2014 most plausibly the increment attributable to \"conservation-incentive pricing\" above the documented 1.6x cost differential, and/or the portion of proceeds redirected to rebates for non-paying customers \u2014 because the facts closely track the defect Capistrano condemned (blending policy-driven demand pricing with cost allocation) and because cross-subsidizing non-payors sits uneasily with \u00a76(b)(2)'s use-restriction and \u00a76(b)(3)'s parcel-proportionality rule. The district's \"not a property-related fee\" fallback is weak post-Bighorn-Desert, and its conservation-mandate defense is unlikely to trump Prop 218's specific textual requirements. The main thing saving part of the surcharge is the genuine, well-documented 1.6x marginal-cost differential, which gives the district a real (if incomplete) cost nexus Capistrano itself treated as legally sufficient when adequately proven.",
  "rationales": [
    "The district's own rate study admits the $0.85 figure is derived 'in part' from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strictly cost allocation \u2014 this is the exact defect Capistrano invalidated, where inclining-tier rates untethered from documented cost differentials failed art. XIII D \u00a76(b)(3).",
    "Directing surcharge revenue to fund rebates that 'mostly benefit customers other than those paying the surcharge' plausibly violates \u00a76(b)(2)'s bar on using fee revenue for any purpose other than that for which it was imposed, and breaks the required nexus between the parcel paying and the service/benefit funded.",
    "Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205 forecloses the district's alternative argument that a usage-based water surcharge is not a 'property-related fee,' pushing the case squarely into \u00a76(b)'s cost-of-service framework rather than allowing an escape into ordinary-tax or non-fee treatment.",
    "Article X, \u00a72's conservation mandate is unlikely to override Prop 218's specific, later-adopted proportionality text, since Capistrano itself rejected a structurally identical 'conservation justifies non-cost-based pricing' defense.",
    "Countervailing: the rate study's documented 1.6x marginal-cost differential (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases) supplies a genuine, quantifiable cost nexus that Capistrano recognized as legally adequate when properly substantiated, so a court could sever/reform rather than fully invalidate, or could uphold the portion of the surcharge that maps onto that 1.6x ratio while striking only the incremental 'incentive' markup and the rebate cross-subsidy."
  ],
  "biggest_uncertainty": "Without the underlying rate-study numbers, it's impossible to tell whether $0.85/hcf actually exceeds what a straightforward application of the 1.6x marginal-cost differential to the base-tier rate would justify \u2014 that quantitative gap (or its absence) is the pivotal fact question a court would need at trial, and it determines whether the whole surcharge, only its increment, or none of it gets struck."
}
```

The anonymized panel summary (statistics + themes, no attribution):

# Delphi Panel — Round 1 Feedback Summary

## 1. Statistics

Panel of 7. Probability that the proposition is correct: median 70.0, mean 71.4, std dev 2.8, range 70.0–78.0, middle 50% of panel between 70.0 and 72.0.

*(For themes below, "higher-probability panelists" refers to the two panelists at 72 and 78; "lower-probability panelists" refers to the five at 70.)*

## 2. Rationale Themes

**Theme A — The "in part conservation-incentive pricing" admission tracks the Capistrano defect (7 of 7).**
Every panelist reasoned that the district's own rate-study language — deriving $0.85 "in part" from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strict cost allocation — maps onto the defect condemned in *Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano*. Characterizations of severity varied within the theme (from "direct doctrinal vulnerability" to "close to a per se violation for the excess increment").

**Theme B — Rebate cross-subsidy as an independent revenue-use/proportionality problem (7 of 7).**
All panelists cited the concession that 55% of proceeds fund rebates mostly benefiting non-payers, invoking some combination of §6(b)(1), (b)(2), and (b)(3). Several framed this as analytically distinct from — and potentially independently dispositive of — the pricing-methodology issue.

**Theme C — The 1.6x documented cost differential as a genuine cost anchor favoring partial rather than total invalidation (7 of 7).**
Every panelist treated the peaking-capacity/drought-reserve cost study as a real cost-of-service basis that makes severance, capping, remand, or partial upholding more likely than wholesale invalidation, and distinguished this record from the agency record in *Capistrano*.

**Theme D — Agency burden of proof under §6(b)(5) (4 of 7).**
Four panelists (three at 70; the panelist at 78) reasoned that the agency's burden of proving compliance, combined with its record concessions, shifts practical litigation risk to the district. One of these grounded the burden/preponderance standard in *Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir*; the others cited the constitutional text directly.

**Theme E — Bighorn forecloses the "not a property-related fee" fallback (5 of 7).**
Five panelists (all at 70) reasoned the district's threshold argument fails under *Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil*, forcing the case to be decided on cost-of-service merits. The two higher-probability panelists did not include this in their rationales (one cited *Bighorn* only in authorities; one cited it for a different proposition — see disagreements).

**Theme F — Judicial deference / no mathematical precision required (3 of 7, skewing higher-probability).**
Both higher-probability panelists (72, 78) plus one panelist at 70 invoked deference authority (*Wilde*, *Griffith*, *Coziahr*) as a countervailing consideration that could salvage the full rate or the district's blended methodology.

**Theme G — 15% conservation-education staffing as a general public benefit problem (3 of 7).**
Three panelists separately flagged the "water ambassador" allocation as funding a general governmental/public-education service rather than service to the paying parcels.

**Theme H — 30% recycled-water debt service as the most defensible tranche (2 of 7).**
Two panelists (one at 70, one at 78) identified the debt-service allocation as squarely traceable to the cost rationale and likely to survive, supporting a "substantial part" rather than "whole" outcome.

**Theme I — Article X, §2 / Water Code §375 cannot override Prop 218's specific text (2 of 7, both at 70).**
Two panelists expressly reasoned that the conservation-mandate defense fails against the later-adopted, more specific §6(b) requirements.

**Cross-cutting uncertainty (flagged by all 7 in their uncertainty statements):** the unquantified gap between $0.85/hcf and what the 1.6x differential would strictly justify, which panelists uniformly identified as potentially outcome-determinative.

## 3. Points of Genuine Disagreement

1. **Identity and holding of *Coziahr v. Otay Water District*.** Panelists cited three incompatible citations: (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 850, (2023) 91 Cal.App.5th 1, and (2024) 103 Cal.App.5th 122. One panelist relied on it affirmatively as deference-to-methodology authority; three others flagged its holding as unverified, and one was uncertain whether it narrows or reinforces *Capistrano*. The panel does not agree on what this case is or says.

2. **How strict *Capistrano* is versus the deference line of cases.** Some panelists treat the "in part incentive-pricing" admission as effectively fatal to the increment above cost (approaching a per se defect); others posit that *Wilde*/*Griffith*-style latitude for non-precise cost allocation could sustain the full $0.85 as a reasonable cost-based approximation. One panelist explicitly framed this as an unresolved doctrinal tension with no controlling Supreme Court decision.

3. **Which *Griffith* case supplies the countervailing authority.** One panelist relied on *Griffith v. City of Santa Cruz* (2012) 207 Cal.App.4th 1130 as tolerating conservation-oriented tiered pricing; four others cited *Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency* (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586. These are different cases with different holdings being used for a similar purpose.

4. **What *Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir* stands for.** One panelist cited it for the agency's burden of proof by a preponderance; another cited it for rate-setting deference and the absence of a mathematical-precision requirement. These are different readings of the same authority.

5. **Whether the rebate program is defensible at all.** Most panelists treat the cross-subsidy concession as essentially indefensible under §6(b)(2)–(3). One panelist (78) identified a specific path by which the district could prevail on it — that rebates reduce system-wide peaking/drought-reserve costs and thereby benefit payors — while concluding the concessions make that a "harder sell."

6. **Whether any partial invalidation necessarily satisfies "in substantial part."** Most panelists assume partial invalidation satisfies the proposition as framed. One panelist (70) expressly disagreed with that assumption, reasoning that a "minor trim" to the rate might arguably not be "substantial," making the unquantified dollar magnitude the crux of whether the proposition is true.

7. **What *Bighorn* is authority for.** Most panelists cited *Bighorn* solely for the property-related-fee threshold; one panelist also cited it (with *Richmond v. Shasta*) as informing the substantive cross-subsidization/proportionality analysis.

## 4. Authorities

*Note: one panelist (at 70) submitted no formal authorities list, though that panelist's rationales expressly referenced Capistrano, Bighorn-Desert, and art. X, §2. Counts below reflect formal authorities lists; parentheticals note that panelist's in-rationale references where applicable.*

**Cited by two or more panelists:**
- Cal. Const. art. XIII D, §6(b)(1)–(5) — 6 (7 including in-rationale reference)
- *Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano* (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 — 6 (7 including in-rationale reference)
- *Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil* (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205 — 6 (7 including in-rationale reference; one panelist spelled "Vejil")
- Cal. Const. art. XIII C / §1(e) (Prop 26) — 6
- Cal. Const. art. X, §2 — 6 (7 including in-rationale reference)
- Water Code §375 — 6
- *Coziahr v. Otay Water District* — 5 (with divergent years and citations; see Disagreement #1; four of five flagged "verify")
- *Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency* (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586 — 4 (two flagged verification of pinpoint applicability)
- *Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District* (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372 — 3
- *Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir* (2020) 9 Cal.5th 1105 — 2

**Cited by only one panelist:**
- *Griffith v. City of Santa Cruz* (2012) 207 Cal.App.4th 1130
- *City of Palmdale v. Palmdale Water Dist.* (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 926
- *Richmond v. Shasta Community Services Dist.* (2004) 32 Cal.4th 409
- *Zolly v. City of Oakland* (2022) 13 Cal.5th 858 (cited with a "verify relevance" flag)
- Gov. Code §66022

---
*Round 2 instructions: Review the themes, disagreements, and authorities above. You may revise or maintain your probability and rationales in light of what other panelists raised.*

Independently reconsider. Do NOT defer to the majority; update only where the summary contains reasons you judge valid, and hold your position where it does not. Explain in response_to_panel which consideration moved you or why none did.

response_to_panel.description: which panel consideration moved you, or why none did

text: {"probability":73,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"Slightly increasing from 70 to 73. A court more likely than not invalidates at least a substantial part of the surcharge — most plausibly (1) the increment of the $0.85 rate attributable to undocumented \\\"conservation-incentive pricing\\\" beyond the proven 1.6x cost differential, and (2) the ~55% of proceeds diverted to rebates that the district concedes mostly benefit non-paying customers, which independently strains §6(b)(2)'s use-restriction and §6(b)(3)'s parcel-proportionality requirement. The unanimous cross-panel convergence on the rebate cross-subsidy issue (Theme B, 7/7) and the burden-of-proof dynamic under §6(b)(5) (Theme D) reinforce rather than undercut my original reasoning; the deference/latitude line of cases (Wilde, Griffith, Coziahr) is a real but, on this record, secondary countervailing force since even the higher-probability panelists who invoked it still landed above the panel median for invalidation. The genuine, documented 1.6x cost differential remains the strongest basis for partial (not total) survival, and the 30% recycled-water debt-service tranche is likely the most defensible piece of the fund allocation.","rationales":["The rebate cross-subsidy problem (55% of revenue funding rebates the district admits mostly benefit non-payors) is, on these facts, close to self-proving under §6(b)(2)'s bar on using fee revenue for any purpose other than that for which the fee was imposed and §6(b)(3)'s parcel-specific proportionality rule; unlike the pricing-methodology question, this defect doesn't depend on unresolved rate-study math — it's a straight structural mismatch between payor class and beneficiary class, which likely satisfies 'substantial part' on its own regardless of how the incentive-pricing question comes out.","The §6(b)(5) burden of proof (agency must show compliance, arguably by preponderance under Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir (2020) 9 Cal.5th 1105 — verify pinpoint) combined with the district's own rate-study concessions ('in part' incentive pricing; rebates benefiting non-payors) means the district is litigating from a documentary hole of its own making, which shifts practical risk toward plaintiffs at summary judgment/trial.","The panel's Theme G (three panelists independently flagging the 15% 'water ambassador' conservation-education staffing as general-public-benefit spending untethered to the paying parcels) adds a third distinct vulnerable tranche beyond the pricing markup and the rebate cross-subsidy, making a finding of 'substantial part' invalidation more overdetermined rather than resting on a single contestable theory.","Countervailing and holding me back from going higher than ~73: the deference line (Wilde, Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586, and the disputed 'Coziahr' citation) genuinely tolerates some imprecision in cost allocation, and the panel's own citation instability around Coziahr (three different years/citations offered) signals real doctrinal uncertainty about how much latitude courts currently give tiered/conservation pricing — a controlling California Supreme Court resolution of the Capistrano/deference tension does not appear to exist.","The documented 1.6x marginal cost differential remains a genuine, quantifiable cost nexus (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases) that Capistrano itself treated as legally sufficient when adequately substantiated, supporting severance/partial-uphold outcomes (especially for the 30% debt-service tranche) rather than wholesale invalidation — this is why I don't move toward 85+ despite the reinforcing themes above."],"biggest_uncertainty":"Two compounding unknowns: (1) the still-unquantified gap between $0.85/hcf and what a strict application of the 1.6x differential to the base rate would yield — this determines whether the pricing-methodology defect is large or marginal; and (2) how a court would characterize the rebate cross-subsidy if the district successfully argues (as the panel's highest-probability dissenter suggested) that rebates reduce system-wide peaking/drought costs and thus indirectly benefit surcharge-payors — if that system-benefit theory gains traction, the 55% rebate tranche could survive on a Richmond v. Shasta-style indirect-benefit theory, which would meaningfully lower the probability of 'substantial part' invalidation.","changed":true,"response_to_panel":"Two things moved me modestly upward from 70 to 73. First, Theme D (the §6(b)(5) burden of proof falling on the district, reinforced by its own damaging concessions) is a consideration I hadn't separately weighted in round 1 and that meaningfully increases practical litigation risk independent of the substantive merits. Second, Theme G (the 15% water-ambassador general-benefit problem) supplies a third independent vulnerability I'd underweighted, making 'substantial part' invalidation more overdetermined. I did not move further upward or let Theme F (deference authorities) pull me down, because the higher-probability panelists who invoked Theme F still landed above the panel median — suggesting the deference line doesn't neutralize the rebate cross-subsidy problem even where panelists took it seriously. I also stayed cautious about the Coziahr citation chaos in the disagreements section (three incompatible citations offered) — I'm treating that authority as unverified and giving it little independent weight until the citation is confirmed, which counsels against a larger upward move on the strength of 'deference' doctrine alone."}

data.position_summary: Slightly increasing from 70 to 73. A court more likely than not invalidates at least a substantial part of the surcharge — most plausibly (1) the increment of the $0.85 rate attributable to undocumented \"conservation-incentive pricing\" beyond the proven 1.6x cost differential, and (2) the ~55% of proceeds diverted to rebates that the district concedes mostly benefit non-paying customers, which independently strains §6(b)(2)'s use-restriction and §6(b)(3)'s parcel-proportionality requirement. The unanimous cross-panel convergence on the rebate cross-subsidy issue (Theme B, 7/7) and the burden-of-proof dynamic under §6(b)(5) (Theme D) reinforce rather than undercut my original reasoning; the deference/latitude line of cases (Wilde, Griffith, Coziahr) is a real but, on this record, secondary countervailing force since even the higher-probability panelists who invoked it still landed above the panel median for invalidation. The genuine, documented 1.6x cost differential remains the strongest basis for partial (not total) survival, and the 30% recycled-water debt-service tranche is likely the most defensible piece of the fund allocation.
rationales: The rebate cross-subsidy problem (55% of revenue funding rebates the district admits mostly benefit non-payors) is, on these facts, close to self-proving under §6(b)(2)'s bar on using fee revenue for any purpose other than that for which the fee was imposed and §6(b)(3)'s parcel-specific proportionality rule; unlike the pricing-methodology question, this defect doesn't depend on unresolved rate-study math — it's a straight structural mismatch between payor class and beneficiary class, which likely satisfies 'substantial part' on its own regardless of how the incentive-pricing question comes out.

The §6(b)(5) burden of proof (agency must show compliance, arguably by preponderance under Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir (2020) 9 Cal.5th 1105 — verify pinpoint) combined with the district's own rate-study concessions ('in part' incentive pricing; rebates benefiting non-payors) means the district is litigating from a documentary hole of its own making, which shifts practical risk toward plaintiffs at summary judgment/trial.

The panel's Theme G (three panelists independently flagging the 15% 'water ambassador' conservation-education staffing as general-public-benefit spending untethered to the paying parcels) adds a third distinct vulnerable tranche beyond the pricing markup and the rebate cross-subsidy, making a finding of 'substantial part' invalidation more overdetermined rather than resting on a single contestable theory.

Countervailing and holding me back from going higher than ~73: the deference line (Wilde, Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586, and the disputed 'Coziahr' citation) genuinely tolerates some imprecision in cost allocation, and the panel's own citation instability around Coziahr (three different years/citations offered) signals real doctrinal uncertainty about how much latitude courts currently give tiered/conservation pricing — a controlling California Supreme Court resolution of the Capistrano/deference tension does not appear to exist.

The documented 1.6x marginal cost differential remains a genuine, quantifiable cost nexus (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases) that Capistrano itself treated as legally sufficient when adequately substantiated, supporting severance/partial-uphold outcomes (especially for the 30% debt-service tranche) rather than wholesale invalidation — this is why I don't move toward 85+ despite the reinforcing themes above.
data.biggest_uncertainty: Two compounding unknowns: (1) the still-unquantified gap between $0.85/hcf and what a strict application of the 1.6x differential to the base rate would yield — this determines whether the pricing-methodology defect is large or marginal; and (2) how a court would characterize the rebate cross-subsidy if the district successfully argues (as the panel's highest-probability dissenter suggested) that rebates reduce system-wide peaking/drought costs and thus indirectly benefit surcharge-payors — if that system-benefit theory gains traction, the 55% rebate tranche could survive on a Richmond v. Shasta-style indirect-benefit theory, which would meaningfully lower the probability of 'substantial part' invalidation.
data.response_to_panel: Two things moved me modestly upward from 70 to 73. First, Theme D (the §6(b)(5) burden of proof falling on the district, reinforced by its own damaging concessions) is a consideration I hadn't separately weighted in round 1 and that meaningfully increases practical litigation risk independent of the substantive merits. Second, Theme G (the 15% water-ambassador general-benefit problem) supplies a third independent vulnerability I'd underweighted, making 'substantial part' invalidation more overdetermined. I did not move further upward or let Theme F (deference authorities) pull me down, because the higher-probability panelists who invoked Theme F still landed above the panel median — suggesting the deference line doesn't neutralize the rebate cross-subsidy problem even where panelists took it seriously. I also stayed cautious about the Coziahr citation chaos in the disagreements section (three incompatible citations offered) — I'm treating that authority as unverified and giving it little independent weight until the citation is confirmed, which counsels against a larger upward move on the strength of 'deference' doctrine alone.
